risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study describes a prospective inclusion of "446 consecutive patients" undergoing liver biopsy and provides no description of any random allocation process. There is no statement of random number generation, randomization, blocking, stratification, or any other sequence-generation method. Comment: Because this was not reported as a randomized allocation study and no truly random sequence is described, the method of sequence generation cannot be judged.

Summary: The study says it prospectively included 446 consecutive patients undergoing liver biopsy, but it does not describe any allocation process or concealment mechanism. Comment: There is no explicit random allocation or concealment method (e.g., central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, or identical containers), so foreknowledge of assignment cannot be ruled out, but this is also not clearly a randomized allocation study.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All biochemical tests and their scores were assessed without knowledge of the liver biopsy results." and "Liver biopsies... were assessed blindly per the METAVIR scoring system by an expert pathologist." Comment: This study was a diagnostic accuracy study rather than an intervention trial, and the reported blinding relates to outcome assessment (biopsy/pathology and biochemical test interpretation), not to participant/personnel allocation. There is no intervention group knowledge that could plausibly influence the main outcomes, which are objective laboratory/imaging measures and histologic fibrosis staging. Therefore performance bias from lack of blinding of participants/personnel is unlikely to affect the study outcomes overall.

Quote: "Liver biopsies, obtained under ultrasonographic guidance and stained with hematoxylin-eosin and Masson's trichrome, were assessed blindly per the METAVIR scoring system by an expert pathologist." Comment: The outcome assessor for the reference standard (liver biopsy fibrosis stage) was an expert pathologist who was explicitly blinded. The primary outcome is histological fibrosis stage, an objective, judgement-based measure, and the paper states it was assessed blindly, so knowledge of allocation could not reasonably bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "This study prospectively included 446 consecutive patients..." and "All patients underwent hematological, biochemical, and virological examinations on the day of their participation." Comment: The paper reports a single cross-sectional diagnostic study in which all included patients had the relevant assessments at the time of participation, and the results section presents outcomes for the full cohort of 446 patients without describing any dropout, withdrawal, or missing outcome data. There is no signal of differential attrition or missingness likely to bias the diagnostic results.
